web.title: Introducing channel search: an easier way to find channels in Slack
web.description: This new search tab screens channel name, channel purpose and frequently discussed topics for the most relevant results.
web.markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://slack.com/blog/productivity/introducing-channel-search-for-slack#main_focusable)

![](https://d34u8crftukxnk.cloudfront.net/slackpress/prod/sites/6/2019-03_Hao_ChannelsSearch_Hero1.jpg?d=500x500&f=inside)

- [Twitter](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=%E2%80%9CIntroducing+channel+search%3A+an+easier+way+to+find+channels+in+Slack%E2%80%9D+via+%40SlackHQ+https%3A%2F%2Fslack.com%2Fblog%2Fproductivity%2Fintroducing-channel-search-for-slack "Twitter")
- [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fslack.com%2Fblog%2Fproductivity%2Fintroducing-channel-search-for-slack "Facebook")
- [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https%3A%2F%2Fslack.com%2Fblog%2Fproductivity%2Fintroducing-channel-search-for-slack "LinkedIn")
- [Copy link](https://slack.com/blog/productivity/introducing-channel-search-for-slack "Copy link")

1 min read

Millions of people work in Slack channels each day. But as the number of channels within your organization grows into the hundreds or even thousands, finding the right one becomes more challenging. While [clear naming conventions](https://get.slack.help/hc/en-us/articles/217626408-create-guidelines-for-channel-names) help with channel organization, it can be tough to discover relevant public channels you’re not yet a part of, especially if you don’t know the exact channel name.

Starting today, [searches in Slack](https://get.slack.help/hc/en-us/articles/202528808-guide-to-search-in-slack-) will help you find not only key conversations and files but also the channels where that information lives.

## How channel search works

Searching for channels is no different than how you search in Slack now. Simply type the terms you’re looking for into the search bar, and fire away. In addition to the “Messages” and “Files” tabs that already exist in the results, you’ll see a new tab named “Channels.” There you’ll find the public channels—along with the private channels you belong to—that are most relevant to your search.

![](https://d34u8crftukxnk.cloudfront.net/slackpress/prod/sites/6/Channel-Search-Mktg-1.2@2X.png)

To further refine your search, you can filter and sort the results by channel type (public, private or archived) or workspace. And with one click, you can preview or join the channels that interest you.

## Channel results, tailored to you

A search isn’t just matching for the channel name but now accounts for other helpful cues, including the channel purpose and frequently discussed topics. It also considers relevance to you and those you work most closely with, improving the chances that you’ll find the right channel.

![](https://d34u8crftukxnk.cloudfront.net/slackpress/prod/sites/6/Channel-Search-Mktg-2@2X.png)

So whether you’re a new employee looking for channels to get up to speed on your work or you just want to check if a channel already exists before creating one, these improvements to search can offer some assurance that you’re in the places—that is, the channels—you need to be.

Channel search is rolling out over the next several days to desktop users. Have feedback? We’d love to hear it! You can [drop us a line](https://slack.com/help/requests/new) or reach us at [@SlackHQ](https://twitter.com/SlackHQ) on Twitter.

- [Channels](https://slack.com/blog/tags/channels)
- [Product updates](https://slack.com/blog/tags/product-updates)

web.markdown:
![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/wD937GyTh5g/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [How To Add Someone To A Private Slack Channel (2026 Easy Guide)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wD937GyTh5g)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [MARK_bro](http://www.youtube.com/@Markmwm)
**Uploaded at**: 2026-02-08T13:58:27-08:00
**Published at**: 2026-02-08T13:58:27-08:00
**Length**: 02:34
**Views**: 8
**Likes**: 0
**Category**: Howto & Style

## Description

```
Learn how to add someone to a private Slack channel step by step using the latest Slack interface. This easy guide walks you through inviting users safely and correctly.

#Slack #SlackTutorial #WorkplaceTools

Timestamps:
0:00 Intro and overview
0:18 Open your Slack workspace
0:39 Select the private channel
1:03 Click the members icon
1:29 Choose add people
1:56 Select existing private channel
2:21 Enter the user’s email

slack, add someone to private slack channel, how to add people to slack, slack private channel invite, invite users to slack channel, slack tutorial for beginners, slack workspace management, slack team collaboration, how to use slack 2026, slack channel permissions, private channel slack guide, slack how to add members, slack software tutorial, workplace communication tools, slack tips and tricks, slack admin guide, slack channel management, slack collaboration guide, slack training video
```

## Transcript

In today's video, I'm going to show you exactly how to add someone to a private Slack channel step by step. This is perfect if you manage a team, work in a company workspace, or need to give someone access to a sensitive conversation without making the channel public. Private channels in Slack are designed for focused and confidential discussions. So, adding the right people the right way is important. If you find this tutorial helpful, make sure to hit the like button, subscribe to the channel, and turn on notifications so you don't miss more easy Slack and software tutorials. All right, let's jump right in. First, make sure you're logged in to your Slack workspace. From the Slack app or browser, look at the left sidebar and navigate to the private channel where you want to add someone. Click on that channel so it opens up in your main workspace view. You must already be a member of the private channel in order to invite someone else. So, double check that you're in the correct channel before moving on. Once the private channel is open, look toward the top right corner of the channel window. You'll see a small person or members icon. This icon represents the list of people who currently have access to the channel. Go ahead and click on that person icon to open the channel details and member options. After clicking the person icon, a panel will appear showing the current members of the private channel. In this panel, look for the option that says add people. This is the option you'll use to invite someone new into the channel. Click on add people to continue. Next, Slack will ask how you want to add the person. Depending on your workspace settings, you may see an option to add them directly to the existing private channel or create a new channel instead. To give them access to the current private channel, choose the option to add them to the existing channel. This ensures they can see past messages depending on your Slack permissions. Now, you'll see a field where you can enter the details of the person you want to invite. Type in the email address of the person you want to invite. Make sure you use the same email address they use for Slack, especially if they already belong to your workspace. Slack may autosuggest users as you type, which helps confirm you're inviting the correct person. Once you've entered the email address or selected the user, click on the add button. Slack may prompt you with a confirmation screen or additional follow-up steps such as confirming access or reviewing permissions. Go through those prompts carefully and complete the process. After you finish these steps, the person will be invited to the private channel. If they're already part of your workspace, they'll be added immediately. If not, they may receive an invitation email and will gain access once they accept it. At that point, they'll be able to view and participate in the private channel just like other members. And that's it. You've successfully added someone to a private Slack channel. This is a simple but essential skill for managing collaboration, keeping conversations organized, and making sure the right people have access to the right information. If this video helped you out, don't forget to like the video and subscribe to the channel for more Slack tips and easy software tutorials. Thanks for watching and I'll see you in the next

# Join a channel

**Who can use this feature?**

- All **members** except **guests**
- Available on [**all plans**](https://slack.com/pricing)

Slack organizes conversations in your workspace into dedicated spaces called [channels](https://slack.com/help/articles/360017938993-What-is-a-channel). Channels bring the right people, tools, and information together for a common purpose — like working on a specific project, or discussing a particular topic with your team. Keep reading to learn how to find and join channels in your workspace.

**Tip:** If you've been invited to work with another company in a channel using Slack Connect, learn how to [accept the invitation](https://slack.com/help/articles/360049769934-Accept-a-Slack-Connect-channel-invitation).

## Join channels

All members (but not guests) can browse and join public channels in their workspace. To join a [private channel](https://slack.com/help/articles/360017938993-What-is-a-channel#public-vsu46-private-channels), you must be [added](https://slack.com/help/articles/201980108-Add-people-to-a-channel) by an existing member of the channel.

DesktopMobile

1. From **Home**, click **Directories**.
2. Click the **Channels** tab.
3. Browse the list of public channels in your workspace, or search for a channel by its name or description.
4. Select a channel from the list to view it.
5. Click **Join Channel**.

1. Tap the **search bar** at the top of your screen.
2. Tap **Channels**.
3. Browse the list of public channels in your workspace, or search for a channel by its name.
4. Tap a channel name to open it, then tap **Join Channel**.

**Note:** If you don't see **Directories**, look for the **Channels** tab in the sidebar instead. You may need to click **More** to find it.

**Awesome!**

Thanks so much for your feedback!

## Description

```
Learn how to add someone to a channel in Slack the right way! In this 2026 step-by-step beginner tutorial, I’ll show you how to invite users to public and private channels, manage members, and keep team communication organized. Perfect for beginners, team leads, and admins using Slack daily.
```

## Transcript

In today's video, I'm going to teach you how to add someone to channel in Slack. If you're collaborating with a team, keeping the right people in the right channels makes conversations organized and easy to find. The good news is that adding someone to a channel takes just a few clicks. And I'll walk you through it clearly so you know exactly what to look for and what to expect. First, open the Slack channel where you want to bring someone in. Make sure you're actually inside that specific channel so the actions you take apply to the correct place. At the top right of the channel header, you'll see a small person icon. This icon represents the channel members. Click that icon to open the member panel. Inside the member panel, look for the option labeled add people. Go ahead and click add people. A small window or field will appear where you can start typing the person's name. As you type, Slack will show suggestions from your workspace. Select the correct person from those suggestions. If you're adding more than one person, you can repeat the process by typing another name and selecting it as well. Once you've chosen everyone you want to add, click add to confirm. Slack will add those people to the channel, and they'll be able to see the conversation and any files shared from that point forward. Depending on your workspace settings, they may get a notification that they've been added so they can jump in right away. A couple of quick tips to keep things smooth. If the channel's private, only certain people can add new members. So, you might need the appropriate permission from the channel admins or owners. If you don't see the person icon or the add people option, check that you're using the desktop or web app with a window wide enough to show the full header and make sure you're a member of the channel yourself. Also, if you're working with similar names, take a second to confirm you've selected the right person in the suggestions list before you click add. That's all there is to it. Open the channel, click the person icon at the top right, choose add people, start entering names, pick the right folks from the suggestions, and click add. In just a moment, your teammates will be in the conversation and ready to collaborate. Thanks for watching.
